content.content:
1Ôćĺ# Architecture: Changelogue
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Overview
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺChangelogue is an event-driven, hybrid-architecture system designed to centralize release discovery, automate validation, manage configurations, and distribute targeted notifications. It combines the high-concurrency performance of a Go-based polling engine with the reasoning capabilities of LLM-based SRE agents. By leveraging PostgreSQL for both persistent storage and message brokering, the system maintains high reliability and transactional consistency within a streamlined, single-binary deployment.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## 1. Tech Stack & Infrastructure
     8Ôćĺ
     9Ôćĺ* **Backend / Engine:** Go (Golang). Chosen for its lightweight concurrency model (Goroutines) to handle simultaneous polling across multiple registries.
    10Ôćĺ* **Frontend / Dashboard:** Next.js (React) with Tailwind CSS. Provides a fast, modern control center for viewing release streams and managing configurations.
    11Ôćĺ* **Database, Queue, & Event Bus:** PostgreSQL.
    12Ôćĺ* *Persistent Storage:* Standard relational tables for release metadata and system configurations.
    13Ôćĺ* *Task Queue:* Utilizes `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` (via the **River** Go library) for robust, concurrent background job processing without race conditions.
    14Ôćĺ* *Pub/Sub:* Utilizes native `LISTEN` and `NOTIFY` for real-time broadcasting to connected clients.
    15Ôćĺ
    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ* **Intelligence Layer:** LLMs (Gemini / OpenAI-compatible) orchestrated via [ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for semantic changelog analysis and autonomous release evaluation.
    18Ôćĺ* **Packaging:** Single binary deployment utilizing Go's `//go:embed` to serve the Next.js static export directly from the Go server.
    19Ôćĺ
    20Ôćĺ## 2. System Design & Abstractions
    21Ôćĺ
    22ÔćĺThe system is decoupled into four primary layers communicating entirely through PostgreSQL using the Transactional Outbox pattern:
    23Ôćĺ
    24Ôćĺ1. **Ingestion Layer** ÔÇö Polling workers and webhook handlers discover new releases from upstream registries.
    25Ôćĺ2. **Notification Routing** ÔÇö River workers send immediate notifications to source-level subscribers on new releases.
    26Ôćĺ3. **Agent Layer** ÔÇö ADK-Go agents research releases, consult context sources, and produce semantic release reports.
    27Ôćĺ4. **Routing & Output** ÔÇö Notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks) deliver alerts via `Sender`.
    28Ôćĺ
    29ÔćĺA **Project** is the central domain entity ÔÇö it groups multiple ingestion sources, context sources, and notification subscriptions under a single tracked piece of software.
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ```mermaid
    32Ôćĺgraph LR
    33Ôćĺ    %% Data Flow
    34Ôćĺ    subgraph Dashboard [API & Dashboard]
    35Ôćĺ        API[REST API] --> |CRUD| PG
    36Ôćĺ        API --> |SSE| Browser[Next.js Dashboard]
    37Ôćĺ    end
    38Ôćĺ
    39Ôćĺ    subgraph Ingestion [Ingestion Layer]
    40Ôćĺ        I_Hub(Docker Hub Poller) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    41Ôćĺ        I_Git(GitHub Atom Feed) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    42Ôćĺ    end
    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ    subgraph Core [Event Bus & Queue - PostgreSQL]
    45Ôćĺ        Engine((Go Engine)) --> |INSERT release + jobs\nTransactional Outbox| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    46Ôćĺ    end
    47Ôćĺ
    48Ôćĺ    subgraph Notification [Notification Routing]
    49Ôćĺ        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver notify_release| NotifyWorker[Notify Worker]
    50Ôćĺ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Slack(Slack)
    51Ôćĺ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Discord(Discord)
    52Ôćĺ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Email(Email)
    53Ôćĺ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Hook(Webhooks)
    54Ôćĺ    end
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ    subgraph AgentLayer [Agent Layer]
    57Ôćĺ        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver agent_run| AgentWorker[Agent Worker]
    58Ôćĺ        AgentWorker --> |ADK-Go| LLM[LLM Provider]
    59Ôćĺ        AgentWorker --> |Research| ContextSources[Context Sources]
    60Ôćĺ        AgentWorker --> |Produce| SemanticRelease[Semantic Release + Report]
    61Ôćĺ    end
    62Ôćĺ
    63Ôćĺ```
    64Ôćĺ
    65Ôćĺ### Entity Relationship Model
    66Ôćĺ
    67Ôćĺ```mermaid
    68ÔćĺerDiagram
    69Ôćĺ    projects ||--o{ sources : "has many"
    70Ôćĺ    projects ||--o{ context_sources : "has many"
    71Ôćĺ    projects ||--o{ semantic_releases : "has many"
    72Ôćĺ    projects ||--o{ agent_runs : "has many"
    73Ôćĺ    sources ||--o{ releases : "produces"
    74Ôćĺ    subscriptions }o--|| notification_channels : "routes to"
    75Ôćĺ    subscriptions }o--o| sources : "source-level"
    76Ôćĺ    subscriptions }o--o| projects : "project-level"
    77Ôćĺ    semantic_releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "composed of"
    78Ôćĺ    releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "contributes to"
    79Ôćĺ
    80Ôćĺ    projects {
    81Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
    82Ôćĺ        string name UK
    83Ôćĺ        text agent_prompt
    84Ôćĺ        jsonb agent_rules
    85Ôćĺ    }
    86Ôćĺ    sources {
    87Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
    88Ôćĺ        uuid project_id FK
    89Ôćĺ        string provider
    90Ôćĺ        string repository UK
    91Ôćĺ        boolean enabled
    92Ôćĺ    }
    93Ôćĺ    releases {
    94Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
    95Ôćĺ        uuid source_id FK
    96Ôćĺ        string version
    97Ôćĺ        jsonb raw_data
    98Ôćĺ    }
    99Ôćĺ    semantic_releases {
   100Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
   101Ôćĺ        uuid project_id FK
   102Ôćĺ        string version
   103Ôćĺ        jsonb report
   104Ôćĺ        string status
   105Ôćĺ    }
   106Ôćĺ    agent_runs {
   107Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
   108Ôćĺ        uuid project_id FK
   109Ôćĺ        uuid semantic_release_id FK
   110Ôćĺ        string trigger
   111Ôćĺ        string status
   112Ôćĺ    }
   113Ôćĺ    subscriptions {
   114Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
   115Ôćĺ        uuid channel_id FK
   116Ôćĺ        string type
   117Ôćĺ        uuid source_id FK
   118Ôćĺ        uuid project_id FK
   119Ôćĺ    }
   120Ôćĺ    notification_channels {
   121Ôćĺ        uuid id PK
   122Ôćĺ        string type
   123Ôćĺ        string name
   124Ôćĺ        jsonb config
   125Ôćĺ    }
   126Ôćĺ```
   127Ôćĺ
   128Ôćĺ### 2.1 The Event-Driven Backbone (PostgreSQL Only)
   129Ôćĺ
   130ÔćĺComponents do not call each other synchronously. Instead, they rely on PostgreSQL to guarantee delivery:
   131Ôćĺ
   132Ôćĺ* **The Transactional Outbox:** When a new release is detected, the ingestion worker writes the metadata to the `releases` table (linked to its `source_id`) and simultaneously enqueues River jobs (notification and/or agent) *within the exact same SQL transaction*. This guarantees no events are ever lost.
   133Ôćĺ* **Real-time Pub/Sub:** Database triggers use `pg_notify` to broadcast lightweight events (e.g., telling the Next.js UI via SSE to refresh) over standard Postgres connections using `LISTEN`.
   134Ôćĺ* **Reliable Queues:** River workers process jobs using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, ensuring exactly-once processing for both notification delivery and agent runs.
   135Ôćĺ* **Project-Centric Model:** All data flows through the `projects` Ôćĺ `sources` Ôćĺ `releases` hierarchy. Subscriptions can attach at the source level (for raw release notifications) or the project level (for semantic release notifications).
   136Ôćĺ
   137Ôćĺ### 2.2 Provider Interfaces (I/O)
   138Ôćĺ
   139ÔćĺAll external integrations are abstracted behind strict Go interfaces.
   140Ôćĺ
   141Ôćĺ* `IIngestionSource`: Standardizes how polling workers fetch data. Adding a new registry (like npm or NuGet) only requires implementing this interface. Each implementation maps to a `source_type` in the database. Current implementations: Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI.
   142Ôćĺ* `Sender`: Standardizes output routing. Each implementation maps to a `type` in the `notification_channels` table (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).
   143Ôćĺ
   144Ôćĺ### 2.3 REST API & Dashboard
   145Ôćĺ
   146ÔćĺThe Go server exposes a RESTful API (`/api/v1`) serving the Next.js dashboard and external consumers:
   147Ôćĺ
   148Ôćĺ* **Resource CRUD:** Projects, sources, subscriptions, notification channels, context sources ÔÇö all manageable through the API.
   149Ôćĺ* **Read-Only Releases:** Releases and semantic releases are queryable but not directly writable via the API ÔÇö they're created exclusively through the ingestion layer and agent runs.
   150Ôćĺ* **Source Operations:** Manual poll trigger (`POST /sources/{id}/poll`) and channel test notifications (`POST /channels/{id}/test`).

Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me provide my findings.

---

## Summary of Codebase Exploration

### 1. Frontend Structure (web/)

**Pages/Routes:**
- `/` ÔÇö Dashboard (home page) ÔÇö main entry point
- `/projects` ÔÇö Projects list view (card & compact modes)
- `/sources` ÔÇö Global sources view across all projects
- `/releases` ÔÇö Releases list
- `/semantic-releases` ÔÇö Semantic releases/analysis view
- `/subscriptions` ÔÇö Notification subscriptions
- `/channels` ÔÇö Notification channels (Slack, Discord, Email, etc.)
- `/todo` ÔÇö Action items/release notification tracking
- `/onboard` ÔÇö Repository dependency scanning & quick onboarding
- `/login` ÔÇö OAuth login page

**Dashboard (Home) - `/app/page.tsx`:**
- Shows **stats cards** (projects, releases, activity metrics)
- **Release trend chart** (time-series visualization)
- **Unified feed** (recent releases across projects)
- **Quick onboard** inline widget (scan repo by URL)
- **Discovery section** (GitHub/Docker Hub search/recommendations)
- Listens to SSE events for real-time updates

### 2. "Source" Data Model (Release Ingestion)

**Source Model** (`/internal/models/source.go`):
A **Source** is what users track ÔÇö a monitored repository/package in an upstream registry (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR, GitLab, PyPI).

**Key fields:**
- `id`, `project_id`, `provider` (docker, github, ecr, etc.)
- `repository` (e.g., "sentioxyz/changelogue")
- `poll_interval_seconds` ÔÇö how often to check for new releases
- `enabled` ÔÇö active/disabled toggle
- `version_filter_include/exclude` ÔÇö regex patterns to filter versions
- `exclude_prereleases` ÔÇö boolean flag
- `last_polled_at`, `last_error` ÔÇö poll status tracking
- `config` ÔÇö provider-specific JSON config

**How sources are used:**
- Users create **projects** (logical groups)
- Then add **sources** to projects (specific packages/repos to track)
- Sources are polled on an interval to fetch new releases
- Releases are ingested and stored, optionally analyzed by LLM agent
- Both sources and releases can be viewed at project-level or globally

### 3. API Endpoints (Sources/Trackers)

**Sources endpoints** (in `/internal/api/server.go` + `/internal/api/sources.go`):
```
GET    /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources       Ôćĺ List sources for project
POST   /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources       Ôćĺ Create new source
GET    /api/v1/sources/{id}                       Ôćĺ Get single source
PUT    /api/v1/sources/{id}                       Ôćĺ Update source config
DELETE /api/v1/sources/{id}                       Ôćĺ Delete source
POST   /api/v1/sources/{id}/poll                  Ôćĺ Manually trigger poll
```

**Projects endpoints:**
```
GET    /api/v1/projects                           Ôćĺ List projects
POST   /api/v1/projects                           Ôćĺ Create project
GET    /api/v1/projects/{id}                      Ôćĺ Get project
PUT    /api/v1/projects/{id}                      Ôćĺ Update project
DELETE /api/v1/projects/{id}                      Ôćĺ Delete project
```

**Other key endpoints:**
- Releases: `GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/releases` ÔÇö releases for project
- Semantic Releases: LLM-analyzed release reports with urgency/recommendations
- Subscriptions: notifications on new releases
- Channels: Slack/Discord/Email notification destinations
- Context Sources: supplementary data sources for agent analysis (separate from ingestion sources)
- Agent: trigger manual analysis runs or view agent activity

### 4. Dashboard/Home Page

**`/app/page.tsx`** shows:
1. **Discovery section** ÔÇö search GitHub/Docker Hub, add repositories quickly
2. **Quick onboard widget** ÔÇö inline form to scan a repo for dependencies
3. **Stats cards** ÔÇö total projects, active sources, releases this week, pending agent work
4. **Release trend chart** ÔÇö releases over time (7/14/30 days)
5. **Unified feed** ÔÇö recent releases across all projects in chronological order
6. **Empty state** ÔÇö if no projects exist, prompts user to create one or import dependencies

All dashboard data refreshes via SSE (Server-Sent Events) when new releases are detected.

### 5. Key Models & Data Flow

**Project hierarchy:**
```
Project (container)
  ÔöťÔöÇ Sources (monitored repositories, e.g., "ethers.js" on npm)
  ÔöťÔöÇ Releases (ingested from sources)
  ÔöťÔöÇ Semantic Releases (LLM-analyzed reports on releases)
  ÔööÔöÇ Context Sources (supplementary data for agent, e.g., GitHub org repos)
```

**Release flow:**
1. User creates Project Ôćĺ adds Sources (Docker/GitHub repos)
2. Ingestion orchestrator polls sources periodically
3. New releases are fetched and stored
4. If agent is enabled, LLM analyzes release for urgency/changelog/recommendations
5. Notifications sent to subscribed channels
6. Todos created for high-urgency releases

---

**File paths for reference:**
- Frontend pages: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/`
- API handlers: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/`
- Models: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/models/`
- Client types/SDK: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/lib/api/`

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary of GitHub OAuth & Token Storage Investigation

### **1. GitHub OAuth Login Flow**

**OAuth Scopes Requested:**
- Only **`read:org`** scope is requested (line 39 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/oauth.go`)
- This is minimalÔÇöjust enough to read org membership for allowlisting

**User Data Stored on Login:**
In the `users` table (lines 191-199 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/db/migrations.go`):
- `id` (UUID, primary key)
- `github_id` (BIGINT, unique)
- `github_login` (VARCHAR)
- `name` (VARCHAR, optional)
- `avatar_url` (TEXT, optional)
- `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps

**OAuth Callback Flow:**
1. User exchanges code Ôćĺ access token (exchanged but **not stored**)
2. Token fetched temporarily to get user info and orgs
3. Token is discarded after login completes
4. Session cookie created instead (server-side in `sessions` table)

### **2. Token Storage - Current State**

**GitHub OAuth tokens:** NOT stored anywhere
- The access token is used once during callback to fetch user/org info, then discarded (line 66-79 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/oauth.go`)
- No `github_token` or similar field in the `users` table

**API Key storage:** Exists but separate from OAuth
- `api_keys` table (lines 124-131 in migrations):
  - `key_hash` (SHA-256 hash, UNIQUE)
  - `key_prefix` (first 12 chars for display)
  - `last_used_at` (for tracking)
- Used for Bearer token authentication, not GitHub OAuth

**Token usage in codebase:**
- `internal/onboard/scanner.go` (lines 47-55) accepts a `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable for repo scanning
- The scanner uses it for GitHub API calls, but this is **environment-level**, not per-user

### **3. User Model & Session Structure**

**User struct** (`/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/user.go`):
```go
type User struct {
    ID          string `json:"id"`          // UUID
    GitHubID    int64  `json:"github_id"`
    GitHubLogin string `json:"github_login"`
    Name        string `json:"name,omitempty"`
    AvatarURL   string `json:"avatar_url,omitempty"`
}
```

**Session storage:**
- `sessions` table: `(id, user_id, expires_at, created_at)` ÔÇö server-side, HttpOnly cookie
- HMAC-signed cookie: `"sessionID.hmacHex"` for tampering protection
- 7-day expiration

### **Key Insights**

1. **No stored GitHub tokens** ÔÇö Current design uses OAuth only for authentication, not authorization to GitHub APIs
2. **Minimal OAuth scope** ÔÇö Only `read:org` (GitHub limits exposure)
3. **Separation of concerns** ÔÇö API keys are independent from user auth; GitHub token access is environment-level via `GITHUB_TOKEN`
4. **Allowlisting via orgs** ÔÇö Can restrict users by GitHub org membership fetched during login
5. **Session pattern is robust** ÔÇö HMAC-signed, server-side, with cleanup

This architecture intentionally **does not bind GitHub tokens to individual users**ÔÇöinstead relying on environment-level access for backend operations and user-level auth via sessions.

Excellent! I have a comprehensive understanding now. Let me create a final summary of my findings: 

## Summary: Ingestion Architecture & Source Configuration

### 1. **Ingestion Providers (6 Supported)**

The system implements 6 upstream registry providers via the `IIngestionSource` interface:

| Provider | File | API Endpoint | Key Details |
|----------|------|--------------|------------|
| **Docker Hub** | `dockerhub.go` | `/v2/repositories/{Repository}/tags/` | Fetches latest 25 tags ordered by `last_updated` |
| **GitHub** | `github.go` | `/repos/{Repository}/releases` | Fetches 30 releases; supports auth token via `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var; captures changelog + prerelease flag |
| **PyPI** | `pypi.go` | `/pypi/{Package}/json` | Uses PyPI JSON API; handles yanked versions; stores release URL in metadata |
| **npm** | `npm.go` | `/{Package}` | Registry API returns all versions with publish timestamps; filters metadata keys (`created`, `modified`) |
| **ECR Public** | `ecr_public.go` | `POST /describeImageTags` (Gallery API) | Pagination support; requires `registry_alias/repo_name` format; captures `imagePushedAt` timestamps |
| **GitLab** | `gitlab.go` | `/api/v4/projects/{ProjectID}/releases` | Supports GitLab token via `GITLAB_TOKEN` env var; URL-encodes project paths; captures prerelease flag |

### 2. **Source Data Model & Configuration**

**Database Table: `sources`** (defined in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/db/migrations.go`, lines 24ÔÇô38)

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sources (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    project_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(id),
    provider VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,              -- "dockerhub", "github", etc.
    repository VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,           -- package/repo name (e.g., "golang" for Docker, "user/repo" for GitHub)
    poll_interval_seconds INT DEFAULT 86400,    -- 24 hours default
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
    config JSONB,                               -- flexible per-provider config
    last_polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    last_error TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    UNIQUE(provider, repository)                -- no duplicate (provider, repo) pairs
);
```

**Recent migrations added filtering:**
- `version_filter_include` (TEXT) ÔÇö include pattern
- `version_filter_exclude` (TEXT) ÔÇö exclude pattern  
- `exclude_prereleases` (BOOLEAN DEFAULT false) ÔÇö skip prerelease versions

### 3. **How Sources are Added & Configured**

**Via REST API** (`/api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources`):
- `POST` creates a new source with provider, repository, poll interval
- `PUT` updates poll interval, enable/disable, filters
- Sources permanently linked to a project (foreign key on `project_id`)

**Factory Function** (`BuildSource` in `loader.go`, lines 87ÔÇô105):
```go
switch sourceType {
case "dockerhub":   return NewDockerHubSource(...)
case "github":      return NewGitHubSource(...)
case "ecr-public":  return NewECRPublicSource(...)
case "gitlab":      return NewGitLabSource(...)
case "pypi":        return NewPyPISource(...)
case "npm":         return NewNpmSource(...)
}
```

### 4. **Polling & Crawling Architecture**

**Orchestrator Flow** (`orchestrator.go`):

1. **Service Startup**: `NewOrchestrator()` creates polling engine with configurable interval (typically every N seconds)
2. **Dynamic Loader**: On each poll cycle, `SourceLoader.LoadEnabledSources()` queries the DB for all enabled sources with their metadata
3. **Schedule Check**: For each source, checks if `now - last_polled_at >= poll_interval_seconds`
4. **Fetch & Persist**: For each due source:
   - Calls `source.FetchNewReleases(ctx)` Ôćĺ HTTP request to upstream API
   - Returns `[]IngestionResult` (version, changelog, metadata, timestamp)
   - Passes results to `Service.ProcessResults()` which calls `store.IngestRelease()` for each

**Transactional Outbox** (`pgstore.go`):
- Each release insertion is wrapped in a transaction with River job enqueueing
- Atomically inserts to `releases` table + enqueues `NotifyJobArgs` to River queue
- Duplicate versions (unique key violation) are skipped with a debug log

**Result Data Model** (`IngestionResult` in `source.go`):
```go
type IngestionResult struct {
    Repository string            // e.g., "golang" (normalization from provider)
    RawVersion string            // e.g., "v1.25.0" (raw tag/version string)
    Changelog  string            // release notes (GitHub, GitLab only)
    Metadata   map[string]string // flexible: release_url, prerelease, etc.
    Timestamp  time.Time         // when the version was released upstream
}
```

### 5. **Database Schema Cascade**

```
projects
  ÔöťÔöÇÔćĺ sources (one provider per source row)
  Ôöé     ÔööÔöÇÔćĺ releases (all versions discovered by this source)
  Ôöé           ÔööÔöÇÔćĺ semantic_release_sources (join table)
  Ôöé                 ÔööÔöÇÔćĺ semantic_releases (AI-correlated releases)
  ÔööÔöÇÔćĺ subscriptions (notification routing)
        ÔööÔöÇÔćĺ notification_channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks)
```

**Key Constraints:**
- `releases` has UNIQUE(source_id, version) ÔÇö prevents duplicate version ingestion
- Release notification is automatic via River's transactional outbox pattern
- Polling sources are never manually written through API; created exclusively via REST source registration

### 6. **Error Handling & Resilience**

- HTTP errors mapped to human-readable messages (`httpStatusError` in `source.go`)
- Rate limiting (HTTP 429) gracefully handled with retry logic in River
- Failed polls update `sources.last_error` field for debugging
- Duplicate releases silently skipped (debug log), not fatal
- Context timeout propagated through all HTTP calls

---

This architecture ensures scalability (horizontal polling), transactional consistency (no event loss), and operator visibility (poll status per source) within a single binary deployment.

## Summary: Ingestion Architecture & Source Configuration

### 1. **Ingestion Providers (6 Supported)**

|----------|------|--------------|------------|
| **Docker Hub** | `dockerhub.go` | `/v2/repositories/{Repository}/tags/` | Fetches latest 25 tags ordered by `last_updated` |
| **GitHub** | `github.go` | `/repos/{Repository}/releases` | Fetches 30 releases; supports auth token via `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var; captures changelog + prerelease flag |
| **PyPI** | `pypi.go` | `/pypi/{Package}/json` | Uses PyPI JSON API; handles yanked versions; stores release URL in metadata |
| **npm** | `npm.go` | `/{Package}` | Registry API returns all versions with publish timestamps; filters metadata keys (`created`, `modified`) |
| **ECR Public** | `ecr_public.go` | `POST /describeImageTags` (Gallery API) | Pagination support; requires `registry_alias/repo_name` format; captures `imagePushedAt` timestamps |
| **GitLab** | `gitlab.go` | `/api/v4/projects/{ProjectID}/releases` | Supports GitLab token via `GITLAB_TOKEN` env var; URL-encodes project paths; captures prerelease flag |

1Ôćĺpackage api
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺimport (
     4Ôćĺ	"net/http"
     5Ôćĺ	"strings"
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ	"github.com/jackc/pgx/v5/pgxpool"
     8Ôćĺ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/auth"
     9Ôćĺ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/ingestion"
    10Ôćĺ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    11Ôćĺ)
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ// Dependencies holds all external dependencies required for registering API routes.
    14Ôćĺtype Dependencies struct {
    15Ôćĺ	DB                    *pgxpool.Pool
    16Ôćĺ	ProjectsStore         ProjectsStore
    17Ôćĺ	ReleasesStore         ReleasesStore
    18Ôćĺ	SubscriptionsStore    SubscriptionsStore
    19Ôćĺ	SourcesStore          SourcesStore
    20Ôćĺ	ChannelsStore         ChannelsStore
    21Ôćĺ	ContextSourcesStore   ContextSourcesStore
    22Ôćĺ	SemanticReleasesStore SemanticReleasesStore
    23Ôćĺ	AgentStore            AgentStore
    24Ôćĺ	TodosStore            TodosStore
    25Ôćĺ	OnboardStore          OnboardStore
    26Ôćĺ	PublicURL             string
    27Ôćĺ	KeyStore              KeyStore
    28Ôćĺ	SessionValidator      SessionValidator
    29Ôćĺ	HealthChecker         HealthChecker
    30Ôćĺ	Broadcaster           *Broadcaster
    31Ôćĺ	NoAuth                bool
    32Ôćĺ	IngestionService      *ingestion.Service
    33Ôćĺ	HTTPClient            *http.Client
    34Ôćĺ}
    35Ôćĺ
    36Ôćĺ// RegisterRoutes registers all API v1 routes on the given ServeMux.
    37Ôćĺfunc RegisterRoutes(mux *http.ServeMux, deps Dependencies) {
    38Ôćĺ	// Public chain: no auth required.
    39Ôćĺ	publicChain := Chain(RequestID, Logger, Recovery)
    40Ôćĺ
    41Ôćĺ	// Authenticated chain: includes auth unless NoAuth is set.
    42Ôćĺ	chain := publicChain
    43Ôćĺ	if !deps.NoAuth {
    44Ôćĺ		chain = Chain(RequestID, Logger, Recovery, RateLimit(10, 20), Auth(deps.KeyStore, deps.SessionValidator))
    45Ôćĺ	}
    46Ôćĺ
    47Ôćĺ	// Projects (CRUD)
    48Ôćĺ	projects := NewProjectsHandler(deps.ProjectsStore)
    49Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.List)))
    50Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Create)))
    51Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Get)))
    52Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Update)))
    53Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Delete)))
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ	// Sources (nested under projects)
    56Ôćĺ	sources := NewSourcesHandler(deps.SourcesStore, deps.IngestionService, deps.HTTPClient)
    57Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.List)))
    58Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Create)))
    59Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Get)))
    60Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Update)))
    61Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Delete)))
    62Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/sources/{id}/poll", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.FetchReleases)))
    63Ôćĺ
    64Ôćĺ	// Context Sources (nested under projects)
    65Ôćĺ	contextSources := NewContextSourcesHandler(deps.ContextSourcesStore)
    66Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/context-sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.List)))
    67Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/context-sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Create)))
    68Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Get)))
    69Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Update)))
    70Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Delete)))
    71Ôćĺ
    72Ôćĺ	// Releases (read-only, nested under sources and projects)
    73Ôćĺ	releases := NewReleasesHandler(deps.ReleasesStore)
    74Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.List)))
    75Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/sources/{id}/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.ListBySource)))
    76Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.ListByProject)))
    77Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.Get)))
    78Ôćĺ
    79Ôćĺ	// Semantic Releases (nested under projects + top-level list)
    80Ôćĺ	semanticReleases := NewSemanticReleasesHandler(deps.SemanticReleasesStore)
    81Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.ListAll)))
    82Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/semantic-releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.List)))
    83Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.Get)))
    84Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.ListSources)))
    85Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.Delete)))
    86Ôćĺ
    87Ôćĺ	// Subscriptions (CRUD)
    88Ôćĺ	subscriptions := NewSubscriptionsHandler(deps.SubscriptionsStore)
    89Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/subscriptions", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.List)))
    90Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/subscriptions", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Create)))
    91Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/subscriptions/batch", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.BatchCreate)))
    92Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/subscriptions/batch", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.BatchDelete)))
    93Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Get)))
    94Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Update)))
    95Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Delete)))
    96Ôćĺ
    97Ôćĺ	// Channels (CRUD + test)
    98Ôćĺ	channels := NewChannelsHandler(deps.ChannelsStore, routing.NewSenders(), deps.PublicURL)
    99Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/channels", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.List)))
   100Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/channels", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Create)))
   101Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Get)))
   102Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Update)))
   103Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Delete)))
   104Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/channels/{id}/test", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Test)))
   105Ôćĺ
   106Ôćĺ	// Agent
   107Ôćĺ	agent := NewAgentHandler(deps.AgentStore)
   108Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/agent/run", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.TriggerRun)))
   109Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/agent/runs", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.ListRuns)))
   110Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/agent-runs/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.GetRun)))
   111Ôćĺ
   112Ôćĺ	// Todos
   113Ôćĺ	todos := NewTodosHandler(deps.TodosStore, deps.PublicURL)
   114Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.List)))
   115Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Get)))
   116Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/acknowledge", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Acknowledge)))
   117Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/resolve", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Resolve)))
   118Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/reopen", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Reopen)))
   119Ôćĺ	// One-click endpoints for notification links (GET so they work as <a href>)
   120Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}/acknowledge", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Acknowledge)))
   121Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}/resolve", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Resolve)))
   122Ôćĺ
   123Ôćĺ	// Onboard (repo dependency scanning)
   124Ôćĺ	ob := NewOnboardHandler(deps.OnboardStore)
   125Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/onboard/scan", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.Scan)))
   126Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.GetScan)))
   127Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.Apply)))
   128Ôćĺ
   129Ôćĺ	// Providers (metadata ÔÇö static, no store needed)
   130Ôćĺ	providers := NewProvidersHandler()
   131Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/providers", chain(http.HandlerFunc(providers.List)))
   132Ôćĺ
   133Ôćĺ	// Discovery (public ÔÇö no auth, proxies external APIs)
   134Ôćĺ	discover := NewDiscoverHandler(deps.HTTPClient, "", "")
   135Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/discover/github", publicChain(http.HandlerFunc(discover.GitHub)))
   136Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/discover/dockerhub", publicChain(http.HandlerFunc(discover.DockerHub)))
   137Ôćĺ
   138Ôćĺ	// SSE events
   139Ôćĺ	events := NewEventsHandler(deps.Broadcaster)
   140Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/events", chain(http.HandlerFunc(events.Stream)))
   141Ôćĺ
   142Ôćĺ	// Health (public ÔÇö no auth middleware)
   143Ôćĺ	health := NewHealthHandler(deps.HealthChecker)
   144Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/health", publicChain(http.HandlerFunc(health.Check)))
   145Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/stats", chain(http.HandlerFunc(health.Stats)))
   146Ôćĺ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/stats/trend", chain(http.HandlerFunc(health.Trend)))
   147Ôćĺ}
   148Ôćĺ
   149Ôćĺ// RegisterAuthRoutes registers OAuth and session endpoints on the mux.
   150Ôćĺ// frontendURL is used in noAuth mode to redirect back to the frontend (may be empty).

1Ôćĺpackage onboard
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺimport (
     4Ôćĺ	"context"
     5Ôćĺ	"encoding/json"
     6Ôćĺ	"fmt"
     7Ôćĺ	"log/slog"
     8Ôćĺ	"time"
     9Ôćĺ
    10Ôćĺ	"github.com/jackc/pgx/v5/pgxpool"
    11Ôćĺ	"github.com/riverqueue/river"
    12Ôćĺ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/models"
    13Ôćĺ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/queue"
    14Ôćĺ)
    15Ôćĺ
    16Ôćĺ// ScanStore is the data access interface for the scan worker.
    17Ôćĺtype ScanStore interface {
    18Ôćĺ	GetOnboardScan(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.OnboardScan, error)
    19Ôćĺ	UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx context.Context, id, status string, results json.RawMessage, scanErr string) error
    20Ôćĺ}
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ// ScanWorker processes scan_dependencies River jobs.
    23Ôćĺtype ScanWorker struct {
    24Ôćĺ	river.WorkerDefaults[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]
    25Ôćĺ	store     ScanStore
    26Ôćĺ	scanner   *Scanner
    27Ôćĺ	extractor *DependencyExtractor
    28Ôćĺ	pool      *pgxpool.Pool
    29Ôćĺ}
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ// NewScanWorker creates a ScanWorker. extractor may be nil if GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set.
    32Ôćĺfunc NewScanWorker(store ScanStore, scanner *Scanner, extractor *DependencyExtractor, pool *pgxpool.Pool) *ScanWorker {
    33Ôćĺ	return &ScanWorker{
    34Ôćĺ		store:     store,
    35Ôćĺ		scanner:   scanner,
    36Ôćĺ		extractor: extractor,
    37Ôćĺ		pool:      pool,
    38Ôćĺ	}
    39Ôćĺ}
    40Ôćĺ
    41Ôćĺfunc (w *ScanWorker) Timeout(_ *river.Job[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]) time.Duration {
    42Ôćĺ	return 3 * time.Minute
    43Ôćĺ}
    44Ôćĺ
    45Ôćĺfunc (w *ScanWorker) Work(ctx context.Context, job *river.Job[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]) error {
    46Ôćĺ	scanID := job.Args.ScanID
    47Ôćĺ	slog.Info("scan worker picked up job", "scan_id", scanID, "attempt", job.Attempt)
    48Ôćĺ
    49Ôćĺ	// Load scan
    50Ôćĺ	scan, err := w.store.GetOnboardScan(ctx, scanID)
    51Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
    52Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("load scan %s: %w", scanID, err)
    53Ôćĺ	}
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ	// Mark processing
    56Ôćĺ	if err := w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "processing", nil, ""); err != nil {
    57Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("update status to processing: %w", err)
    58Ôćĺ	}
    59Ôćĺ
    60Ôćĺ	// Parse repo URL
    61Ôćĺ	owner, repo, err := ParseRepoURL(scan.RepoURL)
    62Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
    63Ôćĺ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    64Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("parse repo URL: %w", err)
    65Ôćĺ	}
    66Ôćĺ
    67Ôćĺ	// Fetch dependency files
    68Ôćĺ	files, err := w.scanner.FetchDependencyFiles(ctx, owner, repo)
    69Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
    70Ôćĺ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    71Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("fetch dependency files: %w", err)
    72Ôćĺ	}
    73Ôćĺ
    74Ôćĺ	slog.Info("found dependency files", "scan_id", scanID, "count", len(files))
    75Ôćĺ
    76Ôćĺ	if len(files) == 0 {
    77Ôćĺ		// No dependency files ÔÇö store empty results
    78Ôćĺ		emptyResults, _ := json.Marshal([]models.ScannedDependency{})
    79Ôćĺ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "completed", emptyResults, "")
    80Ôćĺ		w.notifyScanComplete(ctx, scanID)
    81Ôćĺ		return nil
    82Ôćĺ	}
    83Ôćĺ
    84Ôćĺ	// Extract dependencies via LLM
    85Ôćĺ	if w.extractor == nil {
    86Ôćĺ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, "LLM not configured (set GOOGLE_API_KEY or OPENAI_API_KEY)")
    87Ôćĺ		return river.JobCancel(fmt.Errorf("extractor not configured"))
    88Ôćĺ	}
    89Ôćĺ
    90Ôćĺ	deps, err := w.extractor.Extract(ctx, files)
    91Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
    92Ôćĺ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    93Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("extract dependencies: %w", err)
    94Ôćĺ	}
    95Ôćĺ
    96Ôćĺ	slog.Info("extracted dependencies", "scan_id", scanID, "count", len(deps))
    97Ôćĺ
    98Ôćĺ	results, err := json.Marshal(deps)
    99Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
   100Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("marshal results: %w", err)
   101Ôćĺ	}
   102Ôćĺ
   103Ôćĺ	if err := w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "completed", results, ""); err != nil {
   104Ôćĺ		return fmt.Errorf("update scan completed: %w", err)
   105Ôćĺ	}
   106Ôćĺ
   107Ôćĺ	w.notifyScanComplete(ctx, scanID)
   108Ôćĺ	return nil
   109Ôćĺ}
   110Ôćĺ
   111Ôćĺ// notifyScanComplete sends a pg_notify on the release_events channel.
   112Ôćĺfunc (w *ScanWorker) notifyScanComplete(ctx context.Context, scanID string) {
   113Ôćĺ	payload := fmt.Sprintf(`{"type":"scan_complete","id":"%s"}`, scanID)
   114Ôćĺ	_, err := w.pool.Exec(ctx, "SELECT pg_notify('release_events', $1)", payload)
   115Ôćĺ	if err != nil {
   116Ôćĺ		slog.Error("pg_notify failed", "scan_id", scanID, "err", err)
   117Ôćĺ	}
   118Ôćĺ}
   119Ôćĺ

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a summary report: 

## Summary: Existing Discovery, Suggestion, and Onboarding Features

I've thoroughly analyzed the Changelogue codebase. Here's what already exists for discovering and suggesting repositories/dependencies:

---

### **1. Discovery Section (Frontend)**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/components/dashboard/discovery-section.tsx`

This is a **marquee carousel** on the dashboard home page that shows trending repositories:
- Displays trending GitHub and Docker Hub repositories in a horizontal scrolling ticker
- Shows repo name, star count, and provider icon
- **One-click tracking**: Users can click a "Star + Plus" button to immediately track a repo
  - Creates a new project
  - Adds a source with daily polling interval (86400 seconds)
  - Auto-polls the source once
  - Refreshes the projects list

**Features:**
- Hover to pause scrolling
- Already-tracked repos show a checkmark and are visually de-emphasized
- Caches trending data for 1 hour to reduce API calls
- Shows loading skeleton while fetching

---

### **2. Discovery API (Backend)**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/discover.go`

Two endpoints exposed via `/discover/` routes (no auth required):

#### `GET /api/v1/discover/github`
- **Query params:**
  - `q` ÔÇö custom search query (optional)
  - `language` ÔÇö filter by programming language (optional)
- **Default behavior:** Returns trending GitHub repos from the last 7 days with 10+ stars, sorted by star count
- Returns up to 25 results
- Caches results for 1 hour
- Example response fields: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `language`, `url`, `avatar_url`, `provider`

#### `GET /api/v1/discover/dockerhub`
- **Query params:** `q` ÔÇö search term (defaults to "nginx")
- Returns up to 25 Docker Hub repositories
- Caches results for 1 hour
- Response fields: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `url`, `provider`

---

### **3. Quick Onboard (Dependency Scanner)**

**Frontend:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/onboard/page.tsx`

A dedicated onboarding wizard with 4 steps:

**Step 1: Input**
- User enters GitHub repo URL (accepts `owner/repo` or full HTTPS URL)
- Can be triggered from dashboard quick-onboard bar or via query string `?repo=`

**Step 2: Scanning**
- Shows progress indicators (Queued Ôćĺ Fetching files Ôćĺ Extracting deps)
- Elapsed time counter
- Uses River job queue (async background processing)

**Step 3: Results**
- Table showing detected dependencies with:
  - Checkboxes to select which to track (all selected by default)
  - Dependency name, version, ecosystem badge, upstream repo
  - Dropdown to assign each dependency to existing project or create new one
  - Ecosystem-specific color badges (Go, NPM, PyPI, Cargo, etc.)
- "Track Selected" button to apply selections

**Step 4: Applied**
- Shows success confirmation with count of created sources/projects
- Lists any skipped dependencies
- Links to view projects

**Supported Dependency Files:**
- `go.mod`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`, `Pipfile`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, `Gemfile`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `build.gradle.kts`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, `docker-compose.yaml`

---

### **4. Onboarding Workflow (Backend)**

**Location:**
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/scanner.go` ÔÇö Fetches dependency files from GitHub
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/extractor.go` ÔÇö LLM-powered dependency extraction (Gemini or OpenAI)
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/worker.go` ÔÇö River job worker
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/onboard.go` ÔÇö HTTP handlers

**Process:**
1. **POST `/api/v1/onboard/scan`** Ôćĺ Creates an `OnboardScan` record with status "pending"
   - Prevents concurrent scans for same repo (returns 409 if active scan exists)
   - Enqueues a `ScanDependenciesJobArgs` River job
2. **Worker picks up job** Ôćĺ Transitions to "processing"
   - Parses GitHub repo URL
   - Fetches all dependency files via GitHub API (recursive tree view)
   - Extracts dependencies using LLM prompt (Gemini 2.0 Flash by default, OpenAI 4o-mini as fallback)
   - Stores results as JSON array of `ScannedDependency` objects
   - Updates scan status to "completed" or "failed"
   - Sends PG NOTIFY on `release_events` channel
3. **GET `/api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}`** Ôćĺ Polls scan status (frontend polls every 2 seconds)
4. **POST `/api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`** Ôćĺ User applies selections
   - Takes array of `OnboardSelection` objects
   - For each selection: creates Project + Source (or adds to existing project)
   - Returns counts of created resources and any skipped entries

**LLM Extraction:**
- Uses system prompt to enforce JSON array output (no markdown, no explanations)
- Extracts: name, version, ecosystem, upstream_repo (GitHub best-guess), provider (github/dockerhub/gitlab/ecr_public)
- Supports both Gemini and OpenAI APIs
- Handles markdown-fenced and raw JSON responses

---

### **5. Dashboard Integration**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/page.tsx`

Three discovery/onboarding entry points:
1. **DiscoverySection component** ÔÇö Trending marquee at top (see #1)
2. **Quick Onboard inline bar** ÔÇö Text input + "Scan" button
   - Routes to `/onboard?repo={url}` on Enter or button click
   - Auto-starts scan from query param
3. **DashboardEmptyState** ÔÇö When no projects exist, shows "Create a Project" button linking to `/projects`

---

### **6. Frontend Client API**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/lib/api/client.ts`

Exposed client methods:
```typescript
discover.github(params?: { q?: string; language?: string })
discover.dockerhub(params?: { q?: string })
onboard.scan(repoUrl: string)
onboard.getScan(id: string)
onboard.apply(id: string, selections: OnboardSelection[])
```

---

### **Key Types**

```typescript
// Discovery
DiscoverItem: { name, full_name, description, stars, language?, url, avatar_url?, provider }

// Onboarding
OnboardScan: { id, repo_url, status, results?, error?, created_at, started_at?, completed_at? }
ScannedDependency: { name, version, ecosystem, upstream_repo, provider }
OnboardSelection: { dep_name, upstream_repo, provider, project_id?, new_project_name? }
```

---

### **What This Means for Your Feature**

Ôťů **Already implemented:**
- Full repo dependency scanning (GitHub-only)
- Trending discovery carousel (GitHub + Docker Hub)
- Bulk project/source creation from scan results
- LLM-powered dependency extraction

ÔÜá´ŞĆ **Potential overlaps to avoid:**
- Don't duplicate the Quick Onboard page
- Don't re-implement the trending carousel (reuse DiscoverySection)
- Don't add another repo URL input (use existing onboard flow or discovery)

­čÄ» **Extension points:**
- Add more discovery sources (beyond GitHub/Docker Hub trending)
- Enhance filtering/search in discovery
- Add personalized recommendations (based on existing projects)
- Extend onboarding to support non-GitHub sources

This feature will involve decisions about UI layout and visual placement on the dashboard. Let me offer the visual companion.

Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)

content.content:
1Ôćĺ# Visual Companion Guide 2Ôćĺ 3ÔćĺBrowser-based visual brainstorming companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and options. 4Ôćĺ 5Ôćĺ## When to Use 6Ôćĺ 7ÔćĺDecide per-question, not per-session. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?** 8Ôćĺ 9Ôćĺ**Use the browser** when the content itself is visual: 10Ôćĺ 11Ôćĺ- **UI mockups** ÔÇö wireframes, layouts, navigation structures, component designs 12Ôćĺ- **Architecture diagrams** ÔÇö system components, data flow, relationship maps 13Ôćĺ- **Side-by-side visual comparisons** ÔÇö comparing two layouts, two color schemes, two design directions 14Ôćĺ- **Design polish** ÔÇö when the question is about look and feel, spacing, visual hierarchy 15Ôćĺ- **Spatial relationships** ÔÇö state machines, flowcharts, entity relationships rendered as diagrams 16Ôćĺ 17Ôćĺ**Use the terminal** when the content is text or tabular: 18Ôćĺ 19Ôćĺ- **Requirements and scope questions** ÔÇö "what does X mean?", "which features are in scope?" 20Ôćĺ- **Conceptual A/B/C choices** ÔÇö picking between approaches described in words 21Ôćĺ- **Tradeoff lists** ÔÇö pros/cons, comparison tables 22Ôćĺ- **Technical decisions** ÔÇö API design, data modeling, architectural approach selection 23Ôćĺ- **Clarifying questions** ÔÇö anything where the answer is words, not a visual preference 24Ôćĺ 25ÔćĺA question *about* a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What kind of wizard do you want?" is conceptual ÔÇö use the terminal. "Which of these wizard layouts feels right?" is visual ÔÇö use the browser. 26Ôćĺ 27Ôćĺ## How It Works 28Ôćĺ 29ÔćĺThe server watches a directory for HTML files and serves the newest one to the browser. You write HTML content, the user sees it in their browser and can click to select options. Selections are recorded to a `.events` file that you read on your next turn. 30Ôćĺ 31Ôćĺ**Content fragments vs full documents:** If your HTML file starts with `/.superpowers/brainstorm/` for the session directory. 46Ôćĺ 47Ôćĺ**Note:** Pass the project root as `--project-dir` so mockups persist in `.superpowers/brainstorm/` and survive server restarts. Without it, files go to `/tmp` and get cleaned up. Remind the user to add `.superpowers/` to `.gitignore` if it's not already there. 48Ôćĺ 49Ôćĺ**Launching the server by platform:** 50Ôćĺ 51Ôćĺ**Claude Code (macOS / Linux):** 52Ôćĺ```bash 53Ôćĺ# Default mode works ÔÇö the script backgrounds the server itself 54Ôćĺscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 55Ôćĺ``` 56Ôćĺ 57Ôćĺ**Claude Code (Windows):** 58Ôćĺ```bash 59Ôćĺ# Windows auto-detects and uses foreground mode, which blocks the tool call. 60Ôćĺ# Use run_in_background: true on the Bash tool call so the server survives 61Ôćĺ# across conversation turns. 62Ôćĺscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 63Ôćĺ``` 64ÔćĺWhen calling this via the Bash tool, set `run_in_background: true`. Then read `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` on the next turn to get the URL and port. 65Ôćĺ 66Ôćĺ**Codex:** 67Ôćĺ```bash 68Ôćĺ# Codex reaps background processes. The script auto-detects CODEX_CI and 69Ôćĺ# switches to foreground mode. Run it normally ÔÇö no extra flags needed. 70Ôćĺscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 71Ôćĺ``` 72Ôćĺ 73Ôćĺ**Gemini CLI:** 74Ôćĺ```bash 75Ôćĺ# Use --foreground and set is_background: true on your shell tool call 76Ôćĺ# so the process survives across turns 77Ôćĺscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project --foreground 78Ôćĺ``` 79Ôćĺ 80Ôćĺ**Other environments:** The server must keep running in the background across conversation turns. If your environment reaps detached processes, use `--foreground` and launch the command with your platform's background execution mechanism. 81Ôćĺ 82ÔćĺIf the URL is unreachable from your browser (common in remote/containerized setups), bind a non-loopback host: 83Ôćĺ 84Ôćĺ```bash 85Ôćĺscripts/start-server.sh \ 86Ôćĺ --project-dir /path/to/project \ 87Ôćĺ --host 0.0.0.0 \ 88Ôćĺ --url-host localhost 89Ôćĺ``` 90Ôćĺ 91ÔćĺUse `--url-host` to control what hostname is printed in the returned URL JSON. 92Ôćĺ 93Ôćĺ## The Loop 94Ôćĺ 95Ôćĺ1. **Check server is alive**, then **write HTML** to a new file in `screen_dir`: 96Ôćĺ - Before each write, check that `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` exists. If it doesn't (or `.server-stopped` exists), the server has shut down ÔÇö restart it with `start-server.sh` before continuing. The server auto-exits after 30 minutes of inactivity. 97Ôćĺ - Use semantic filenames: `platform.html`, `visual-style.html`, `layout.html` 98Ôćĺ - **Never reuse filenames** ÔÇö each screen gets a fresh file 99Ôćĺ - Use Write tool ÔÇö **never use cat/heredoc** (dumps noise into terminal) 100Ôćĺ - Server automatically serves the newest file 101Ôćĺ 102Ôćĺ2. **Tell user what to expect and end your turn:** 103Ôćĺ - Remind them of the URL (every step, not just first) 104Ôćĺ - Give a brief text summary of what's on screen (e.g., "Showing 3 layout options for the homepage") 105Ôćĺ - Ask them to respond in the terminal: "Take a look and let me know what you think. Click to select an option if you'd like." 106Ôćĺ 107Ôćĺ3. **On your next turn** ÔÇö after the user responds in the terminal: 108Ôćĺ - Read `$SCREEN_DIR/.events` if it exists ÔÇö this contains the user's browser interactions (clicks, selections) as JSON lines 109Ôćĺ - Merge with the user's terminal text to get the full picture 110Ôćĺ - The terminal message is the primary feedback; `.events` provides structured interaction data 111Ôćĺ 112Ôćĺ4. **Iterate or advance** ÔÇö if feedback changes current screen, write a new file (e.g., `layout-v2.html`). Only move to the next question when the current step is validated. 113Ôćĺ 114Ôćĺ5. **Unload when returning to terminal** ÔÇö when the next step doesn't need the browser (e.g., a clarifying question, a tradeoff discussion), push a waiting screen to clear the stale content: 115Ôćĺ 116Ôćĺ ```html 117Ôćĺ 118Ôćĺ
119Ôćĺ

Continuing in terminal...

120Ôćĺ
121Ôćĺ ``` 122Ôćĺ 123Ôćĺ This prevents the user from staring at a resolved choice while the conversation has moved on. When the next visual question comes up, push a new content file as usual. 124Ôćĺ 125Ôćĺ6. Repeat until done. 126Ôćĺ 127Ôćĺ## Writing Content Fragments 128Ôćĺ 129ÔćĺWrite just the content that goes inside the page. The server wraps it in the frame template automatically (header, theme CSS, selection indicator, and all interactive infrastructure). 130Ôćĺ 131Ôćĺ**Minimal example:** 132Ôćĺ 133Ôćĺ```html 134Ôćĺ

Which layout works better?

135Ôćĺ

Consider readability and visual hierarchy

136Ôćĺ 137Ôćĺ
138Ôćĺ
139Ôćĺ
A
140Ôćĺ
141Ôćĺ

Single Column

142Ôćĺ

Clean, focused reading experience

143Ôćĺ

145Ôćĺ
146Ôćĺ
B
147Ôćĺ
148Ôćĺ

Two Column

149Ôćĺ

Sidebar navigation with main content

# Visual Companion Guide Browser-based visual brainstorming companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and options. ## When to Use Decide per-question, not per-session. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?** **Use the browser** when the content itself is visual: - **UI mockups** ÔÇö wireframes, layouts, navigation structures, component designs - **Architecture diagrams** ÔÇö system components, data flow, relationship maps - **Side-by-side visual comparisons** ÔÇö comparing two layouts, two color schemes, two design directions - **Design polish** ÔÇö when the question is about look and feel, spacing, visual hierarchy - **Spatial relationships** ÔÇö state machines, flowcharts, entity relationships rendered as diagrams **Use the terminal** when the content is text or tabular: - **Requirements and scope questions** ÔÇö "what does X mean?", "which features are in scope?" - **Conceptual A/B/C choices** ÔÇö picking between approaches described in words - **Tradeoff lists** ÔÇö pros/cons, comparison tables - **Technical decisions** ÔÇö API design, data modeling, architectural approach selection - **Clarifying questions** ÔÇö anything where the answer is words, not a visual preference A question *about* a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What kind of wizard do you want?" is conceptual ÔÇö use the terminal. "Which of these wizard layouts feels right?" is visual ÔÇö use the browser. ## How It Works The server watches a directory for HTML files and serves the newest one to the browser. You write HTML content, the user sees it in their browser and can click to select options. Selections are recorded to a `.events` file that you read on your next turn. **Content fragments vs full documents:** If your HTML file starts with `/.superpowers/brainstorm/` for the session directory. **Note:** Pass the project root as `--project-dir` so mockups persist in `.superpowers/brainstorm/` and survive server restarts. Without it, files go to `/tmp` and get cleaned up. Remind the user to add `.superpowers/` to `.gitignore` if it's not already there. **Launching the server by platform:** **Claude Code (macOS / Linux):** ```bash # Default mode works ÔÇö the script backgrounds the server itself scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` **Claude Code (Windows):** ```bash # Windows auto-detects and uses foreground mode, which blocks the tool call. # Use run_in_background: true on the Bash tool call so the server survives # across conversation turns. scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` When calling this via the Bash tool, set `run_in_background: true`. Then read `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` on the next turn to get the URL and port. **Codex:** ```bash # Codex reaps background processes. The script auto-detects CODEX_CI and # switches to foreground mode. Run it normally ÔÇö no extra flags needed. scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` **Gemini CLI:** ```bash # Use --foreground and set is_background: true on your shell tool call # so the process survives across turns scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project --foreground ``` **Other environments:** The server must keep running in the background across conversation turns. If your environment reaps detached processes, use `--foreground` and launch the command with your platform's background execution mechanism. If the URL is unreachable from your browser (common in remote/containerized setups), bind a non-loopback host: ```bash scripts/start-server.sh \ --project-dir /path/to/project \ --host 0.0.0.0 \ --url-host localhost ``` Use `--url-host` to control what hostname is printed in the returned URL JSON. ## The Loop 1. **Check server is alive**, then **write HTML** to a new file in `screen_dir`: - Before each write, check that `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` exists. If it doesn't (or `.server-stopped` exists), the server has shut down ÔÇö restart it with `start-server.sh` before continuing. The server auto-exits after 30 minutes of inactivity. - Use semantic filenames: `platform.html`, `visual-style.html`, `layout.html` - **Never reuse filenames** ÔÇö each screen gets a fresh file - Use Write tool ÔÇö **never use cat/heredoc** (dumps noise into terminal) - Server automatically serves the newest file 2. **Tell user what to expect and end your turn:** - Remind them of the URL (every step, not just first) - Give a brief text summary of what's on screen (e.g., "Showing 3 layout options for the homepage") - Ask them to respond in the terminal: "Take a look and let me know what you think. Click to select an option if you'd like." 3. **On your next turn** ÔÇö after the user responds in the terminal: - Read `$SCREEN_DIR/.events` if it exists ÔÇö this contains the user's browser interactions (clicks, selections) as JSON lines - Merge with the user's terminal text to get the full picture - The terminal message is the primary feedback; `.events` provides structured interaction data 4. **Iterate or advance** ÔÇö if feedback changes current screen, write a new file (e.g., `layout-v2.html`). Only move to the next question when the current step is validated. 5. **Unload when returning to terminal** ÔÇö when the next step doesn't need the browser (e.g., a clarifying question, a tradeoff discussion), push a waiting screen to clear the stale content: ```html

Continuing in terminal...

``` This prevents the user from staring at a resolved choice while the conversation has moved on. When the next visual question comes up, push a new content file as usual. 6. Repeat until done. ## Writing Content Fragments Write just the content that goes inside the page. The server wraps it in the frame template automatically (header, theme CSS, selection indicator, and all interactive infrastructure). **Minimal example:** ```html

Which layout works better?

Consider readability and visual hierarchy

A

Single Column

Clean, focused reading experience

B

Two Column

Sidebar navigation with main content

``` That's it. No ``, no CSS, no `

Good, the visual companion is running. Now let me start with the first clarifying question. Since this is a conceptual/scope question, I'll use the terminal.

Great, visual companion is live at **http://localhost:52861** ÔÇö open that in your browser. I'll use it when we get to layout/UI decisions.

Now let me make sure I understand what you're envisioning. I explored the codebase and here's what already exists:

- **Discovery carousel** ÔÇö shows trending GitHub/Docker Hub repos on the dashboard
- **Quick onboard scanner** ÔÇö paste a GitHub repo URL, it scans dependency files with LLM, lets you bulk-track them
- **GitHub OAuth** ÔÇö stores `github_id`, `github_login`, but **does not store the user's access token** (it's discarded after login)

Your idea adds two new **personalized** dimensions:
1. **Starred repos** ÔÇö show the user's GitHub stars as tracking suggestions
2. **Recently active repos** ÔÇö crawl repos the user works on and suggest their dependencies

Here's my first question:

**For the "starred repos" feature ÔÇö do you mean the user's own GitHub starred repositories (repos they've clicked the star button on), or something else?** And for the "recently working projects" ÔÇö do you mean the user's own GitHub repos (ones they own/contribute to), and then scanning those for their dependency files (like `go.mod`, `package.json`, etc.)?

message.content: yes, the user's own GitHub starred repositories and repos they own/contribute to, resue the same llm dependencies analyze

content.text:
Good, that confirms the scope. Now an important technical question.

Currently, the GitHub OAuth token is **discarded after login** ÔÇö only `read:org` scope is requested, and the token isn't stored. To fetch a user's starred repos, their own repos, and repo contents for dependency scanning, we'd need to:

1. **Request additional OAuth scopes** (at minimum `read:user` for stars, `repo` or `public_repo` for accessing repo contents)
2. **Store the GitHub access token** in the database tied to the user

**How do you feel about the scope of GitHub access?** Should we:

- **A) Public data only** ÔÇö request just enough to read the user's public stars and public repos (lower friction, less permissions)
- **B) Include private repos** ÔÇö request `repo` scope to also see private repos and their dependencies (more useful, but users may hesitate to grant broad access)

message.content: Let's start with A then in the future we will support B

**When should the GitHub data be fetched?** Fetching starred repos and user repos + scanning dependencies is not instant. Should it happen:

content.text:
Here's a summary of decisions so far, then my proposed approaches.

## Decisions So Far

- **OAuth**: Store the GitHub token, request public-only scopes (`read:user`, `public_repo` deferred ÔÇö just public API access)
- **Placement**: Replace trending carousel with personalized suggestions for authenticated users; fall back to trending for unauthenticated
- **Trigger**: On demand ÔÇö user clicks to fetch
- **Layout**: Two tabs ÔÇö "Your Stars" and "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies tab flow**: Show user's repos as a checklist Ôćĺ they pick which to scan Ôćĺ reuse existing LLM dependency extraction
- **Stars tab**: Show starred repos with one-click track

## Proposed Approaches

### Approach 1: Minimal ÔÇö GitHub public API, no token storage (Recommended)

Use the **unauthenticated GitHub API** (or a single server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`) to fetch user data by username (which we already have as `github_login`). No OAuth scope changes needed.

- `GET /users/{login}/starred` ÔÇö public stars (public repos only)
- `GET /users/{login}/repos` ÔÇö public repos

**Pros**: No OAuth changes, no token storage, no security surface. Ships fast.
**Cons**: Can't see private stars or private repos. Rate limited to 60 req/hr unauthenticated (but server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` gives 5000/hr). Only public data.

### Approach 2: Store OAuth token, expand scopes

Change OAuth flow to request `read:user` + `public_repo` scopes. Store the encrypted token in the `users` table. Use per-user tokens for GitHub API calls.

- Full access to user's starred repos (including private stars)
- Full access to user's repos (public only with `public_repo`)
- Per-user rate limits (5000/hr per user)

**Pros**: Per-user rate limits, can see private stars, future-proof for private repo support (scope B).
**Cons**: Need to store tokens securely, handle token expiry/refresh, more complex auth flow, migration needed.

### Approach 3: Hybrid ÔÇö public API now, token storage later

Start with Approach 1 for the initial release. Build the feature around `github_login` and public APIs. Later add token storage as an opt-in "Connect GitHub" step for enhanced access.

**Pros**: Ships fastest with zero auth changes. Clean upgrade path. Separation of concerns.
**Cons**: Two implementation passes. Some users might expect private repo access.

---

**My recommendation: Approach 1 (Minimal).** Since you said public data only for now, we don't actually need to store the token at all. The user's `github_login` is already in the DB ÔÇö we can fetch their public stars and public repos using the server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` that the onboard scanner already uses. Zero OAuth changes, zero migrations for token storage. When you're ready for private repo support later, we add token storage then.

What do you think?
Good. Let me present the design section by section.

Updated task #5 status

content.text:
## Design Section 1: Backend API

Two new endpoints under `/api/v1/suggestions/`:

**`GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`**
- Requires authenticated session
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates (GitHub returns 30 per page, we fetch up to ~100)
- Returns list of repos with: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `language`, `url`, `avatar_url`
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (same pattern as existing discovery cache)

**`GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`**
- Requires authenticated session
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Returns list of user's public repos with: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `language`, `url`, `pushed_at`
- Used to populate the repo picker for dependency scanning

**Dependency scanning** ÔÇö reuses existing `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` + `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` + `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`. No new endpoints needed for the actual scanning.

No new database tables or migrations needed.

Does this section look right so far?

## Design Section 2: Frontend Flow

The personalized suggestions replace the trending carousel for authenticated users. Here's the interaction flow:

**Initial state (not yet fetched):**
- Where the trending carousel currently sits, show a card: "Discover repos from your GitHub" with two tabs: "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- Each tab has a button to fetch data ("Load your starred repos" / "Load your repos")

**Stars tab ÔÇö after fetch:**
- Grid/list of starred repos, each with: name, description, stars count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos shown with a checkmark (same pattern as existing discovery carousel)
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source, same as discovery carousel logic)
- Pagination or "Load more" if >30 results

**Dependencies tab ÔÇö multi-step flow:**
1. Click "Load your repos" Ôćĺ fetches repo list from `/api/v1/suggestions/repos`
2. Shows checkboxes list of repos sorted by last pushed. User selects which to scan.
3. Click "Scan selected" Ôćĺ calls `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` for each selected repo
4. Shows scanning progress (reuse existing onboard progress UI)
5. Results grouped by source repo: "From `myapp` Ôćĺ express 4.18, pg 8.11, ..." with checkboxes
6. "Track Selected" button Ôćĺ calls existing `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`

**Fallback for unauthenticated users:**
- Show the existing trending discovery carousel (no change)

Does this flow make sense?

## Design Section 3: Architecture & Components

Let me show you a mockup of what this looks like.

content.text:
Check **http://localhost:52861** ÔÇö I've mocked up both tabs:

1. **Stars tab** ÔÇö grid of starred repos with Track buttons, already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
2. **Dependencies tab** ÔÇö repo picker with checkboxes sorted by last push, "Scan Selected" button

**Component breakdown:**
- `SuggestionsSection` ÔÇö replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Contains tab state and delegates to child components.
- `StarsTab` ÔÇö fetches `/api/v1/suggestions/stars`, renders repo cards with track buttons. Reuses the one-click track logic from the existing discovery carousel.
- `DepsTab` ÔÇö multi-step: repo picker Ôćĺ scanning progress Ôćĺ results with track checkboxes. Reuses the existing onboard scan API and progress UI patterns.

**Backend:**
- New file `internal/api/suggestions.go` ÔÇö two handlers (`handleSuggestStars`, `handleSuggestRepos`) that proxy GitHub public API with caching.
- Registered on the existing router, auth-gated.

**No new database tables. No migrations. No OAuth changes.**

Does this architecture look right? Any changes to the mockup?

## Design Section 4: Edge Cases & Error Handling

A few things to handle:

- **GitHub rate limiting**: Server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` gives 5000 req/hr. Cache results per-user for 1 hour. If rate limited, show a friendly "GitHub API limit reached, try again later" message.
- **User with no stars**: Show an empty state ÔÇö "You haven't starred any repos on GitHub yet" with a link to explore trending instead.
- **User with no public repos**: Same pattern ÔÇö "No public repos found" with suggestion to use the quick onboard scanner instead.
- **Scanning failures**: Individual repo scans can fail (private repo mistakenly listed as public, empty repo, no dependency files). Show per-repo status ÔÇö success/failed/no dependencies found. Don't block other scans.
- **Already tracked filtering**: Both tabs filter out repos/dependencies that are already tracked as sources. The stars tab dims them with a checkmark (shown in mockup). The dependencies results do the same.
- **Multiple concurrent scans**: The existing onboard scanner already prevents concurrent scans for the same repo (409 conflict). For multi-repo scanning, we queue them sequentially or in parallel depending on how many ÔÇö cap at 5 concurrent scans to avoid flooding.

Anything you'd add or change here?

input.content:
# Personalized GitHub Suggestions ÔÇö Design Spec

## Overview

Add personalized repo/dependency suggestions to the dashboard for GitHub-authenticated users. Replaces the generic trending discovery carousel with two tabs: "Your Stars" (user's starred repos) and "Your Dependencies" (scan user-selected repos for dependencies). Uses public GitHub API only ÔÇö no OAuth changes needed.

## Decisions

- **Data access**: Public GitHub API via server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` (no per-user token storage, no OAuth scope changes)
- **Placement**: Replaces trending carousel for authenticated users; unauthenticated users see existing trending carousel
- **Trigger**: On demand ÔÇö user clicks to fetch each tab's data
- **Layout**: Two tabs ÔÇö "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies flow**: User picks repos from checklist Ôćĺ scan selected Ôćĺ review & track results
- **Dependency scanning**: Reuses existing onboard scan pipeline (LLM extraction via `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan`)

## Backend API

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates up to ~100 results (GitHub returns 30/page)
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (keyed by `github_login`)
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, stars, language, url, avatar_url, provider: "github" }`

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Caches per-user for 1 hour
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, language, url, pushed_at }`

### Dependency scanning

Reuses existing endpoints ÔÇö no new endpoints needed:
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` ÔÇö start scan for a repo
- `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` ÔÇö poll scan status
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply` ÔÇö apply selections (create projects + sources)

## Frontend Components

### SuggestionsSection

Top-level component that replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Manages tab state ("stars" | "deps"). Falls back to `DiscoverySection` for unauthenticated users.

### StarsTab

- Initial state: "Load your starred repos" button
- After fetch: grid of repo cards with name, description, star count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source + auto-polls, same logic as discovery carousel)
- "Show more" pagination

### DepsTab

Multi-step flow:

1. **Repo picker**: "Load your repos" button Ôćĺ fetches repo list Ôćĺ checkboxes sorted by `pushed_at` desc Ôćĺ "Scan Selected (N)" button
2. **Scanning**: Progress indicators per repo (reuses onboard progress UI patterns). Cap at 5 concurrent scans.
3. **Results**: Dependencies grouped by source repo (e.g., "From myapp Ôćĺ express, lodash, pg"). Checkboxes to select which to track. Dropdown to assign to existing project or create new one.
4. **Applied**: Success confirmation with counts (reuses onboard applied state)

## Edge Cases

- **GitHub rate limiting**: 5000 req/hr with server-side token. Cache 1hr per user. Show friendly error if rate limited.
- **No stars / no repos**: Empty state messages with suggestion to try trending discovery or quick onboard instead.
- **Scan failures**: Per-repo status (success/failed/no deps). Failed scans don't block others.
- **Already tracked**: Both tabs filter/dim repos and dependencies already tracked as sources.
- **Concurrent scans**: Cap at 5 concurrent per user. Existing 409 conflict handling for duplicate repo scans.

## Files to Create/Modify

### New files
- `internal/api/suggestions.go` ÔÇö two handlers + GitHub API client functions + caching
- `web/components/dashboard/suggestions-section.tsx` ÔÇö top-level tabbed component
- `web/components/dashboard/stars-tab.tsx` ÔÇö stars tab with repo cards
- `web/components/dashboard/deps-tab.tsx` ÔÇö dependencies tab with multi-step flow

### Modified files
- `internal/api/server.go` ÔÇö register new `/api/v1/suggestions/` routes
- `web/lib/api/client.ts` ÔÇö add `suggestions.stars()` and `suggestions.repos()` client methods
- `web/app/page.tsx` ÔÇö conditionally render `SuggestionsSection` vs `DiscoverySection` based on auth state

## Non-goals (future work)

- Private repo access (requires storing per-user OAuth tokens, expanded scopes)
- Auto-fetch on first login
- Recommendation ranking / ML-based suggestions
- Non-GitHub providers (GitLab, Bitbucket stars/repos)

Replaces the generic trending discovery carousel with two tabs: "Your Stars" (user's starred repos) and "Your Dependencies" (scan user-selected repos for dependencies). Uses public GitHub API only ÔÇö no OAuth changes needed.

## Decisions

- **Data access**: Public GitHub API via server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` (no per-user token storage, no OAuth scope changes)
- **Placement**: Replaces trending carousel for authenticated users; unauthenticated users see existing trending carousel
- **Trigger**: On demand ÔÇö user clicks to fetch each tab's data
- **Layout**: Two tabs ÔÇö "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies flow**: User picks repos from checklist Ôćĺ scan selected Ôćĺ review & track results
- **Dependency scanning**: Reuses existing onboard scan pipeline (LLM extraction via `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan`)

## Backend API

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates up to ~100 results (GitHub returns 30/page)
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (keyed by `github_login`)
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, stars, language, url, avatar_url, provider: "github" }`

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Caches per-user for 1 hour
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, language, url, pushed_at }`

### Dependency scanning

Reuses existing endpoints ÔÇö no new endpoints needed:
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` ÔÇö start scan for a repo
- `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` ÔÇö poll scan status
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply` ÔÇö apply selections (create projects + sources)

## Frontend Components

### SuggestionsSection

Top-level component that replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Manages tab state ("stars" | "deps"). Falls back to `DiscoverySection` for unauthenticated users.

### StarsTab

- Initial state: "Load your starred repos" button
- After fetch: grid of repo cards with name, description, star count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source + auto-polls, same logic as discovery carousel)
- "Show more" pagination

### DepsTab

Multi-step flow:

1. **Repo picker**: "Load your repos" button Ôćĺ fetches repo list Ôćĺ checkboxes sorted by `pushed_at` desc Ôćĺ "Scan Selected (N)" button
2. **Scanning**: Progress indicators per repo (reuses onboard progress UI patterns). Cap at 5 concurrent scans.
3. **Results**: Dependencies grouped by source repo (e.g., "From myapp Ôćĺ express, lodash, pg"). Checkboxes to select which to track. Dropdown to assign to existing project or create new one.
4. **Applied**: Success confirmation with counts (reuses onboard applied state)

## Edge Cases

- **GitHub rate limiting**: 5000 req/hr with server-side token. Cache 1hr per user. Show friendly error if rate limited.
- **No stars / no repos**: Empty state messages with suggestion to try trending discovery or quick onboard instead.
- **Scan failures**: Per-repo status (success/failed/no deps). Failed scans don't block others.
- **Already tracked**: Both tabs filter/dim repos and dependencies already tracked as sources.
- **Concurrent scans**: Cap at 5 concurrent per user. Existing 409 conflict handling for duplicate repo scans.

## Files to Create/Modify

### New files
- `internal/api/suggestions.go` ÔÇö two handlers + GitHub API client functions + caching
- `web/components/dashboard/suggestions-section.tsx` ÔÇö top-level tabbed component
- `web/components/dashboard/stars-tab.tsx` ÔÇö stars tab with repo cards
- `web/components/dashboard/deps-tab.tsx` ÔÇö dependencies tab with multi-step flow

### Modified files
- `internal/api/server.go` ÔÇö register new `/api/v1/suggestions/` routes
- `web/lib/api/client.ts` ÔÇö add `suggestions.stars()` and `suggestions.repos()` client methods
- `web/app/page.tsx` ÔÇö conditionally render `SuggestionsSection` vs `DiscoverySection` based on auth state

## Non-goals (future work)

- Private repo access (requires storing per-user OAuth tokens, expanded scopes)
- Auto-fetch on first login
- Recommendation ranking / ML-based suggestions
- Non-GitHub providers (GitLab, Bitbucket stars/repos)

EOF
)"
